schema.region: One of: Americas, Europe, East Asia, Southeast Asia, Middle East & Africa, Oceania.
schema.colMultiplier: Cost of living multiplier on user's USD baseline spending. 1.00 = US. Derived from Numbeo Cost of Living Index by Country, relative to US (US = 68.8 -> 1.00).
schema.annualHealthcareUSD: Additive baseline annual healthcare cost in USD for a retired adult / small family. Heuristic midpoint.
schema.withdrawalTaxRate: Approximate effective tax rate on retirement withdrawals for a moderate-income retiree. Heuristic — not a substitute for individualized tax modeling.
schema.swr: Suggested safe withdrawal rate baseline for this country.
schema.safetyScore: Global Peace Index 2025 score (1.0 = most peaceful, ~3.5 = least). Lower is safer.
schema.safetyRank: GPI 2025 rank out of 163 countries. Lower is safer.
schema.confidence: high | medium | low — reflects how well-supported the parameter set is by public data.
schema.dominantFaith: Dominant religious tradition (cultural majority, not necessarily practicing).
schema.visaDifficulty: Retirement / long-term residency visa difficulty for US citizens: easy | medium | hard | closed.
schema.englishLevel: How widely English is spoken: widespread | urban (cities/tourist areas) | limited (need to learn the local language).
schema.yearsToCitizenship: Standard residency-based naturalization years for a US citizen with no heritage / fast-track path. Lower is faster.
schema.dualCitizenshipAllowed: Whether the country permits dual citizenship with the US (false = must renounce US, e.g. Japan, Korea, Singapore).
schema.foreignerPropertyOwnership: Foreign residential property ownership: allowed (freehold) | restricted (zones/condos only) | leasehold-only | closed.

safety.name: Global Peace Index 2025 (Wikipedia)
safety.note: GPI is published annually each June. Scores typically drift only modestly year-over-year except in countries with active conflict.

costOfLiving.name: Numbeo Cost of Living Index by Country 2026
costOfLiving.note: colMultiplier = country COL Index / US COL Index (68.8). Numbeo's index excludes rent; for retirees with paid-off housing this is the more accurate baseline.

countries.name: United States
countries.residencyNote: Default base case. Healthcare estimate assumes pre-Medicare ACA marketplace coverage for a retired couple.
caveats: Withdrawal tax rate assumes mixed taxable/IRA/Roth at moderate income. Heavy pre-tax skews higher.
Healthcare baseline excludes long-term care.
Safety score is a national average; varies enormously by state and city.

countries.name: Canada
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa for non-residents. Family-sponsored or skilled-worker pathways. Permanent residency required for provincial healthcare access.

sources.name: Canada Revenue Agency tax treaty with US

caveats: Provincial healthcare excellent for residents; wait times for non-emergency procedures can be significant.
Tax rate is federal + provincial average; varies by province (AB lowest, QC highest).

countries.name: Mexico
countries.residencyNote: Temporary Resident visa (4 years renewable) at ~$4,300/mo income or ~$72k savings. Permanent Resident after 4 years or with higher thresholds.
caveats: Safety varies dramatically by state; expat hubs (San Miguel, Mérida, Lake Chapala) much safer than national average.
Mexico taxes worldwide income for residents (>183 days/yr).

countries.name: Costa Rica
countries.residencyNote: Pensionado: $1,000/mo lifetime pension. Rentista: $2,500/mo for 2 yrs or $60k deposit. Inversionista: $150k investment. No standing army.

sources.name: Costa Rica Ministerio de Hacienda (foreign-source income)

caveats: Foreign-source income generally exempt from Costa Rican income tax.
Caja (CCSS) enrollment mandatory for residents; private supplements common.

countries.name: Panama
countries.residencyNote: Pensionado visa: $1,000/mo lifetime pension ($750 if buying $100k+ property). Very generous discounts for pensionado holders.
caveats: Territorial tax system: foreign-source income not taxed.
US dollar is legal tender alongside the balboa; no currency risk for US retirees.

countries.name: Colombia
countries.residencyNote: Retirement (M-Pensionado) visa: ~$1,000/mo pension. Medellín and Bogotá popular with expats.
caveats: Safety improved significantly since 2002 but varies by region; cartel and ELN activity in rural areas.
Residents taxed on worldwide income at progressive rates.

countries.name: Ecuador
countries.residencyNote: Retirement visa: ~$1,400/mo passive income. Uses US dollar (dollarized economy). Cuenca and Vilcabamba popular expat destinations.
caveats: Security has deteriorated significantly in coastal cities (Guayaquil, Manta) since 2023. Highlands remain safer.
Dollarized economy removes currency risk for US retirees.

countries.name: Uruguay
countries.residencyNote: Permanent residency requires demonstrating sufficient income. 11-year tax holiday on foreign-source income for new residents (extended in 2020).
caveats: Most peaceful country in South America by most measures.
Tax holiday rules have changed over time; verify current rules with a local tax specialist.

countries.name: Chile
countries.residencyNote: Temporary residency for retirees with sufficient passive income. Path to permanent residency after 1 year of temporary status.

sources.name: SII Chile (6-year foreign-source exemption for new residents)

caveats: Foreign-source income exempt for first 6 years of residency (extendable by 3); then worldwide.
Healthcare via Isapre (private) or Fonasa (public) for residents.

countries.name: Argentina
countries.residencyNote: Rentista visa available with proof of sufficient passive income. Citizenship after 2 years of residency.
caveats: High inflation and currency controls historically; situation evolving rapidly under recent governments.
Effective tax for foreigners with dollar-denominated assets often informal; treat the rate as nominal.

countries.name: Portugal
countries.residencyNote: D7 passive-income visa: ~$870/mo for primary applicant. NHR program revised in 2024; replacement IFICI regime applies to qualifying high-skilled workers but not general retirees. EU residency after 5 years.
caveats: NHR 10% rate on foreign pensions phased out for new entrants after Mar 2024. New retirees default to standard progressive rates.
Lisbon and Porto significantly more expensive than smaller cities.

countries.name: Spain
countries.residencyNote: Non-Lucrative Visa: ~$2,800/mo passive income. Citizenship after 10 years (2 for Sephardic Jews / Ibero-Americans). EU residency after 5 years.

sources.name: Agencia Tributaria Spain (worldwide income, wealth tax)

caveats: Spain taxes worldwide income progressively (19-47%). Wealth tax exists regionally — Madrid 100% rebate, Catalonia and others do not.
Beckham Law (24% flat) applies to workers only, not retirees.

countries.name: Italy
countries.residencyNote: Elective Residence Visa: ~$3,000/mo passive income, no work. 7% flat tax on all foreign income for retirees relocating to qualifying towns (under 20k pop) in Southern Italy, for up to 9 years.

sources.name: Agenzia delle Entrate (7% retiree flat tax)

caveats: 7% rate requires moving to a qualifying small Southern town. Outside that, taxes are progressive (23-43%).
Tax rate shown assumes the 7% regime.

countries.name: France
countries.residencyNote: Visiteur (long-stay visitor) visa for retirees with sufficient income (~$1,500/mo) and private health insurance. EU residency after 5 years.

sources.name: France Direction Générale des Finances Publiques (US-France treaty)

caveats: US-France tax treaty generally favorable for US-source retirement income (often taxed only in US).
Mutuelle (top-up) insurance recommended for full coverage.

countries.name: Germany
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. Family-based, freelancer (Freiberufler), or self-employed visa pathways. EU residency after 5 years.

sources.name: Bundeszentralamt für Steuern (worldwide taxation for residents)

caveats: Mandatory public health insurance for residents (GKV) or private (PKV) — coverage requirement is non-negotiable.
Residents taxed on worldwide income.

countries.name: Netherlands
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. DAFT (Dutch-American Friendship Treaty) provides entrepreneur pathway. EU residency after 5 years.
caveats: Box 3 wealth tax on investment assets — complex and subject to ongoing reform.
Mandatory health insurance for residents.

countries.name: Switzerland
countries.residencyNote: Forfait fiscal (lump-sum taxation) available to non-Swiss retirees in many cantons — tax based on lifestyle expenses rather than income.
caveats: Forfait fiscal makes Switzerland surprisingly competitive for high-net-worth retirees despite high COL.
Mandatory private health insurance (LaMal) is expensive but high-quality.

countries.name: Austria
countries.residencyNote: Settlement Permit for financially independent persons (Niederlassungsbewilligung) requires demonstrating sufficient passive income.
caveats: Austria taxes worldwide income for residents at progressive rates.
Permit quotas are tight — quota refreshes January 1st each year.

countries.name: Belgium
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa; income-based residency pathways exist. EU residency after 5 years.
caveats: One of the higher overall tax burdens in Europe.
Mutuelle health system requires registration.

countries.name: Ireland
countries.residencyNote: Stamp 0 long-term residency for retirees of independent means: ~$56k/yr individual or $100k/yr couple. EU residency after 5 years.
caveats: Among the most peaceful countries globally — top 5 consistently.
Dublin meaningfully more expensive than rest of country.

countries.name: United Kingdom
countries.residencyNote: No retirement visa post-Brexit. Ancestry visa, family visa, or skilled worker route. ILR (settlement) after 5 years.

sources.name: HMRC UK (residence and tax treaty with US)

caveats: NHS available for residents after settlement; private supplement common.
Investor visa was closed in 2022.

countries.name: Czech Republic
countries.residencyNote: Long-term residence for purpose of family reunification, business, or study. No dedicated retirement visa.
caveats: Prague significantly more expensive than rest of country.
EU residency after 5 years of long-term residence.

countries.name: Poland
countries.residencyNote: Temporary residence for financially independent persons. Permanent residence after 5 years. EU citizenship pathway after 8-10 years.
caveats: Polish flat tax at 12-32% with relatively simple structure.
Warsaw and Kraków more expensive than smaller cities.

countries.name: Slovenia
countries.residencyNote: Temporary residence for sufficient means. EU residency after 5 years.
caveats: Among the top-10 most peaceful countries globally.
Small country with strong outdoor lifestyle; Ljubljana the only large city.

countries.name: Croatia
countries.residencyNote: Temporary residence for retirees with sufficient passive income. Digital nomad visa also available. EU member since 2013; Schengen since 2023.
caveats: Coastal areas (Dalmatia, Istria) significantly more expensive than interior.
Joined Eurozone in 2023.

countries.name: Greece
countries.residencyNote: Foreign pensioner program: 7% flat tax on all foreign-source income for 15 years for new tax residents who relocate from abroad. Golden Visa also available for property investment.

sources.name: AADE Greece (7% foreign pension flat tax)

caveats: 7% rate is significant for US retirees — among the most favorable EU regimes available.
Healthcare via EOPYY; private supplements common.

countries.name: Sweden
countries.residencyNote: No retirement visa. Family or work-based pathways. Permanent residency after 5 years; citizenship after 8.
caveats: High tax burden but excellent public services.
Long winters can be difficult; consider time-zone partitioning if relocating.

countries.name: Norway
countries.residencyNote: Not in EU — Norway is EEA. Retirement requires demonstrating sufficient means; no specific retirement visa.
caveats: High COL even by European standards.
Public healthcare excellent; very limited private sector.

countries.name: Denmark
countries.residencyNote: No retirement visa. Family reunification or work-based pathways. EU permanent residency after 5 years.
caveats: Highest effective tax burden in the dataset; offset by strong public services.
Consistently top-10 most peaceful country.

countries.name: Hungary
countries.residencyNote: Investor (Golden Visa) program relaunched in 2024 with $260k+ real-estate fund or $1.05M property investment. White card for digital nomads.
caveats: Simple 15% flat tax on personal income.
Budapest meaningfully more expensive than rest of country.

countries.name: Iceland
countries.residencyNote: Outside EU but in EEA. Long-term residence for sufficient means; no specific retirement visa.
caveats: World's most peaceful country (GPI 2025 rank 1).
Very high COL; remote with limited tourist-season weather.

countries.name: Estonia
countries.residencyNote: Digital Nomad Visa for remote workers. No dedicated retirement visa; family or income-based pathways. EU residency after 5 years. E-Residency provides administrative access but not physical residency.
caveats: Flat 20% personal income tax (rising to ~22% in coming years).
Strong digital infrastructure; small country with cool climate.

countries.name: South Korea
countries.residencyNote: F-2 long-term residency or F-5 permanent residency. No dedicated retirement visa; pathway requires prior residency, family ties, or qualifying income.
caveats: COL multiplier (0.90) is higher than commonly assumed — Seoul is expensive for major appliances and housing.
Visa path is the practical constraint for most US citizens, not the math.

countries.name: Japan
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. Long-term resident or spouse-of-Japanese visa are common pathways. Highly Skilled Professional visa accelerates PR for qualifying workers.

sources.name: Wikipedia: Health care system in Japan

caveats: Japan taxes worldwide income for residents after 5 of last 10 years in country.
Inheritance tax exposure for long-term residents is significant — not modeled here.

countries.name: Taiwan
countries.residencyNote: Taiwan Gold Card available for qualifying professionals; APRC (permanent residency) achievable after sustained residency. Foreign-source income receives favorable treatment.
caveats: Foreign-source income generally favorable; jurisdiction-specific.
Cross-strait political risk not modeled — outside the scope of a FIRE calculator.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.residencyNote: LTR visa: $80k+ passive income or $1M assets, 10-year stay. Retirement (O-A/O-X) visa: 50+, savings + monthly income proof. Thailand Privilege (Elite) visa: $14k-$70k+ membership.
caveats: 2024 rule change: foreign income remitted in any year is taxable for residents. Major reversal of prior loophole.
Southern provinces near Malaysian border have insurgent activity — not relevant to typical expat areas but reflected in national GPI.

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.residencyNote: Malaysia My Second Home (MM2H): tiered Silver/Gold/Platinum with fixed deposit $150k-$1M and monthly income proof. 5-20 year renewable. English widely spoken.
caveats: Foreign-source income generally exempt from Malaysian tax for individuals; verify current rules.
MM2H rules revised multiple times in recent years.

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. Most expats use 1- or 5-year tourist/business visa cycles or marriage-based residency. Investor visas with capital commitment.
caveats: Visa pathway is the dominant constraint, not the math.
Tax enforcement on resident worldwide income varies in practice.

countries.name: Singapore
countries.residencyNote: Global Investor Programme requires $7.5M+ commitment. Long Term Visit Pass (LTVP) for parents of Singapore residents. No general retirement visa.
caveats: Territorial tax: foreign-source income generally not taxed for individuals.
Among the most peaceful countries in Asia but also one of the highest-COL.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.residencyNote: SRRV (Special Resident Retiree's Visa) at $10k-$50k deposit depending on age. Indefinite stay; multiple entries.
caveats: SRRV is one of the easier retirement visas in Asia.
Safety varies significantly by island/region; Mindanao restricted for some nationalities.

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.residencyNote: Second Home Visa (B211B / KITAS) requires $130k+ deposit in Indonesian bank or property. Bali in particular has strong expat infrastructure.
caveats: Second Home Visa requirements have changed multiple times since 2022.
Bali, Yogyakarta, and Jakarta have different COL profiles.

countries.name: Cambodia
countries.residencyNote: ER (Retirement) visa renewable annually with minimal documentation; one of the easier visas in the region.
caveats: Visa is easy but private healthcare is limited; major medical needs typically require travel to Bangkok or Singapore.
USD widely accepted alongside the riel.

countries.name: Laos
countries.residencyNote: Long-term visa via business setup or property investment. Less developed expat infrastructure than neighbors.
caveats: COL is approximate; Numbeo does not currently list Laos in its country index.
Healthcare infrastructure significantly less developed than Thailand or Vietnam.

countries.name: United Arab Emirates
countries.residencyNote: Retirement visa available to applicants 55+ with $545k property, $272k savings, or $54k/yr income. Renewable every 5 years.

sources.name: UAE Federal Tax Authority (no personal income tax)

caveats: No personal income tax — major draw for high-net-worth retirees.
Mandatory private health insurance; Dubai/Abu Dhabi requirements differ slightly.

countries.name: Israel
countries.residencyNote: Aliyah (Law of Return) for Jewish individuals provides immediate citizenship and 10-year exemption on foreign income/capital gains.
caveats: 10-year Aliyah tax exemption is one of the most generous regimes globally for those eligible.
GPI 2025 reflects active regional conflict — score is significantly elevated vs historical baseline.

countries.name: Turkey
countries.residencyNote: Citizenship by investment available with $400k+ property purchase (since 2022). Residence permit pathways for retirees.
caveats: Severe currency volatility (lira) — significant FX risk for non-lira earners with lira expenses.
Citizenship-by-investment program has changed thresholds multiple times since 2017.

countries.name: Morocco
countries.residencyNote: Long-term residence card available after legal stay. No specific retirement visa but income-based pathways accessible.
caveats: Foreign-source pension income enjoys an 80% deduction — effective tax rate can be quite low.
French-speaking; varying levels of English in tourist areas.

countries.name: South Africa
countries.residencyNote: Retired Person's Permit: ~$2,000/mo passive income or $260k+ in income-generating assets.
caveats: Crime is a real factor; expat areas (Cape Town suburbs, Stellenbosch) typically safer than national average.
Private medical aid (insurance) effectively required given strain on public system.

countries.name: Mauritius
countries.residencyNote: Retired Non-Citizen Permit: $1,500/mo income or $18k+ initial deposit, then $1,500/mo. 10-year renewable. Multilingual (English/French/Creole).
caveats: 15% flat personal income tax — among the simplest tax systems globally.
Island isolation; major medical issues may require travel to South Africa or Réunion.

countries.name: Australia
countries.residencyNote: Investor Retirement Visa (subclass 405) was closed in 2018. Current options: family-sponsored or skilled migration. Permanent residency via state nomination or family.
caveats: No dedicated retirement visa makes legal residency the practical constraint.
Worldwide income taxed at progressive rates; capital gains have 50% discount for long-held assets.

countries.name: New Zealand
countries.residencyNote: Parent Retirement Resident Visa: $740k investment + sufficient income. Active Investor Plus visa for high-net-worth applicants.
caveats: Third most peaceful country globally (GPI 2025).
Investor visa thresholds revised under recent governments — verify current requirements.
